body: Great article! I've been using these principles in my projects and they really make a difference.
I think you missed mentioning the importance of edge functions in modern web dev.
This is exactly what I needed to read today. Bookmarked for reference.
Could you elaborate more on HATEOAS? It's one of the most misunderstood REST constraints.
TypeScript has been a game changer for our team. No more runtime type errors!
I still prefer vanilla JS for small projects. TypeScript adds too much ceremony for simple scripts.
Every developer should read this. Accessibility is not optional.
Can you recommend any automated accessibility testing tools?
Docker Compose makes multi-service development so much easier. Great intro!
I've had issues with Docker performance on macOS. Any tips?
Finally someone explains when to use Grid vs Flexbox clearly!
I use Grid for page layouts and Flexbox for component internals. Works great.
GraphQL has improved our API ergonomics significantly. Great introduction.
What about the N+1 query problem in GraphQL? That's a major concern.
The useReducer + useContext pattern is underrated. Great for medium complexity state.
Custom hooks are where the real power is. Thanks for the deep dive!
Code reviews saved us from shipping a critical security bug last week. Can't stress this enough.
I appreciate the focus on making reviews positive, not adversarial. Culture matters.
Streams are confusing at first but incredibly powerful once you get the hang of them.
The pipe method makes stream composition so elegant. Great examples.
tRPC looks amazing but I worry about vendor lock-in with TypeScript-only clients.
We migrated from REST to tRPC and cut our API boilerplate by 60%.
The testing pyramid is a great mental model. Quality > quantity.
I'd add that snapshot testing should be used sparingly. They create brittle tests.
Trunk-based development has worked well for our team of 8. Fewer merge conflicts.
GitFlow makes more sense for projects with formal release cycles though.
Lazy loading images alone improved our LCP by 40%. Huge win!
Don't forget about route-based code splitting too. It's equally impactful.
Our design system saved us hundreds of hours. Worth the upfront investment.
How do you handle component versioning in a design system?
Cold starts are the real killer for serverless. We moved latency-sensitive routes back to always-on.
Serverless works great for event-driven workloads. Not everything needs sub-100ms responses.
WebSockets made our collaboration feature possible. Nothing else comes close for real-time.
Have you looked into Server-Sent Events? They're simpler for one-way real-time updates.
Zustand has become my go-to for state management. Simple, performant, and minimal API.
For server state, React Query is unbeatable. It handles caching, retries, and background refetching beautifully.
Naming things is one of the hardest parts of programming. These conventions help a lot.
The single responsibility principle for functions is something I wish I'd learned earlier.
We added an index and our query went from 3 seconds to 5 milliseconds. Incredible.
Don't forget that indexes slow down writes. It's always a trade-off.
Micro-frontends added complexity but gave our 5 teams true independence.
Module federation in webpack 5 makes micro-frontends much more practical.
Helmet.js should be in every Express app. Security headers are too easy to forget.
Rate limiting is often overlooked. It should be one of the first things you add to a public API.
The animation examples are beautiful. CSS has come so long way.
Always prefer transform and opacity for animations — they're GPU-accelerated.
Documentation is the most undervalued skill in software engineering. Period.
I write docs before writing code now. It forces me to think through the design.
GitHub Actions has completely replaced our Jenkins setup. So much simpler.
The matrix strategy for testing across Node versions is incredibly useful.
Container queries are a game changer. Finally, truly component-driven responsive design.
The clamp() function for fluid typography is pure magic. No more breakpoint-based font sizes.
I built a scaffolding CLI with Inquirer.js and it saved our team hours of boilerplate.
Chalk v5 is ESM-only which caused some headaches. Worth mentioning in the article.
PKCE should be the default for all SPAs. The implicit flow is deprecated for good reason.
OAuth is confusing but this is one of the clearest explanations I've read.
Turborepo has been fantastic for our monorepo. Build caching alone was worth the migration.
The CI time with a monorepo can be brutal without proper caching and selective builds.
Custom error classes make debugging so much easier. I use them in every project now.
Error boundaries in React saved us from showing blank screens to users. Essential pattern.
Edge computing is the future of low-latency applications. Great overview.
Cloudflare Workers have been incredibly reliable for us. The DX is excellent.
Inline validation is so much better than showing all errors on submit.
React Hook Form + Zod is the best combo for forms in React right now.
Cache invalidation is one of the two hard things in computer science. Good practical advice here.
stale-while-revalidate is my favorite cache strategy. Best of both worlds.
Fast feedback loops are the most important thing for developer productivity. Spot on.
Clear error messages alone can reduce support tickets by 50%. Invest in them.
Day.js is my go-to. Same API as Moment but only 2KB. Perfect replacement.
The Temporal API can't come to browsers soon enough. Native date handling will be so much better.
Sliding window rate limiting is the most fair algorithm. Token bucket can be too bursty.
Don't forget to rate limit by API key too, not just IP address.
Conditional breakpoints are the most underused DevTools feature. They're incredibly powerful.
The Performance tab helped me find a memory leak that had been bugging us for months.
PWAs have gotten much better but push notifications still feel limited compared to native.
We shipped a PWA for our internal tool and adoption was higher than our native app.
Pure functions make testing so much easier. No mocking needed.
Composition over inheritance is the most important lesson from functional programming.
We use PostgreSQL for everything. JSONB columns give us the flexibility of NoSQL when we need it.
SQLite is surprisingly capable for small to medium applications. Don't overlook it.
Shadow DOM is great for encapsulation but makes styling tricky. CSS parts help but aren't perfect.
Lit makes web components development so much more ergonomic. Highly recommend it.
QUIC being UDP-based was a bold choice. The results speak for themselves though.
HTTP/3 adoption is growing fast. All major browsers support it now.
We migrated 200k lines to TypeScript over 6 months. Gradual migration is the way to go.
The strict mode in tsconfig catches so many bugs. Enable it from day one if you can.
pnpm's disk space savings are significant if you work on many projects. Worth trying.
Bun as a package manager is blazing fast but the ecosystem is still catching up.
D3.js gives the most control for dashboards but the learning curve is steep.
For most dashboards, Chart.js or Recharts is more than enough. Don't over-engineer it.
Layout thrashing is one of the most common performance issues. Great explanation of the rendering pipeline.
The will-change CSS property is powerful but should be used sparingly.
Event sourcing adds complexity but gives you a complete audit trail. Worth it for financial apps.
CQRS doesn't have to be complex. Even simple read/write separation helps at scale.
The best documentation I've seen uses progressive disclosure. Don't dump everything at once.
Writing is thinking. The act of writing documentation often reveals design flaws.
We switched to dotenv-vault for team secret sharing. Much better than passing .env files around.
envalid is excellent for validating environment variables at startup. Fail fast!
Variable fonts are the future. One file that covers all weights and widths.
font-display: swap is essential for good perceived performance. Don't block rendering on fonts.
